prompt: Choice B: The median nerve (C5–T1) travels with the brachial artery in the arm, passes between the pronator teres heads, and enters the hand via the carpal tunnel. It supplies motor innervation to most forearm flexors and the thenar muscles via the recurrent branch (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, flexor pollicis brevis), and provides sensory input to the lateral palm and palmar aspects of the lateral 3½ digits (plus dorsal distal tips). Injury leads to thenar weakness, loss of thumb opposition, and sensory deficits; carpal tunnel compression causes nocturnal paresthesias and thenar atrophy. Because it contains critical motor fibers for hand function, a biopsy here risks disabling motor impairment and is inappropriate when aiming to avoid motor dysfunction.

Choice B: Astigmatism is caused by an irregular curvature of the cornea or lens, resulting in distorted or blurred vision at all distances. The abnormal shape leads to light focusing at multiple points on the retina, compromising image clarity. This condition does not cause eye misalignment, diplopia, or abnormal cover-uncover test findings. Astigmatism commonly presents with visual blurring, headaches, and eyestrain, but does not produce diplopia or strabismus. It is corrected with cylindrical lenses that compensate for the irregular refractive power of the cornea or lens. Choice C: Hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too short anteroposteriorly or the cornea has insufficient curvature, causing light to focus behind the retina. Patients with hyperopia see distant objects more clearly than near objects, often leading to difficulty with close work. Children may compensate with increased accommodation, but this can cause eyestrain or headaches. Hyperopia does not cause ocular misalignment or diplopia, but uncorrected severe hyperopia can predispose to accommodative esotropia, a specific form of strabismus, which may overlap with this presentation but is not the general mechanism described here. Clinical presentation is typically blurry near vision, not double vision or shifting ocular fixation. Choice D: Myopia (nearsightedness) is due to an elongated eyeball or excessive corneal curvature, resulting in light focusing in front of the retina. This causes distant objects to appear blurry while near vision remains clear. Myopia does not cause strabismus or issues with binocular alignment, and patients do not exhibit abnormal findings on cover- uncover testing. Severe myopia increases the risk of retinal detachment due to elongation of the globe, but does not account for the described diplopia or misalignment. Clinical features center around blurry distance vision and frequent squinting, not double vision or depth perception loss. Bottom line: Strabismus is a disorder of ocular alignment that results in binocular vision loss, diplopia, and impaired depth perception, and can cause amblyopia if untreated. Differentiating strabismus from refractive errors like astigmatism, hyperopia, and myopia is critical, as only strabismus presents with eye misalignment and abnormal cover-uncover test. answer: Shoulder Dermatomal distribution. Although proximal ureteral colic originates around T10–T12 and can cause flank/loin pain, it does not typically project to the true anterior thigh distribution of the femoral nerve. In ureteric stones, migration to the distal ureter shifts pain toward L1–L2 dermatomes, favoring the groin rather than the anterior thigh, making this choice incorrect in this clinical setting. Choice C: Medial thigh pain follows the obturator nerve (L2–L4), often implicated in obturator hernias, pelvic sidewall pathology, or hip osteoarthritis causing adductor spasm. This distribution is not the canonical referral pattern for ureteral colic, which tracks sympathetic afferents from T10–L2 and shifts flank → lower quadrant → groin as the stone descends. Because the obturator territory is not the typical target for distal ureteral pain, medial thigh radiation is not the best answer here. Choice D: The perineum is innervated predominantly by the pudendal nerve (S2–S4) and is a hallmark referral zone for anorectal disease, pelvic floor disorders, perineal abscess, and urogenital conditions centered in the distal urethra or external genitalia. Ureteral visceral afferents do not travel with sacral parasympathetics; instead they accompany sympathetic fibers to T10–L2, explaining why perineal radiation is not expected in ureteric stone pain. Given this distinct neuroanatomy, perineal localization is inconsistent with the scenario of migrating ureteral colic. Choice E: The shoulder is a common site of referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation via the phrenic nerve (C3–C5), seen in conditions such as subphrenic abscess, splenic rupture, or peritonitis involving the diaphragm. This dermatomal distribution is entirely unrelated to ureteral innervation, which travels via sympathetic fibers from T10–L2. Because the neuroanatomic pathways are completely different, shoulder radiation would not occur in ureteral colic, making this choice entirely incorrect for this patient’s presentation. Bottom line: Ureteral colic from nephrolithiasis produces severe, colicky flank pain that changes location as the stone migrates, reflecting the T10–L2 visceral afferent pathway. When the stone reaches the distal ureter, pain is referred to the groin via shared innervation with the genitofemoral nerve (L1–L2). Anatomy Neurology (Innervation of Other organ systems) None

This compresses the ipsilateral CN III, leading to a dilated pupil, ptosis, and eye positioned "down and out" due to unopposed action of the lateral rectus and superior oblique muscles. Early pupillary dilation is a HY finding on USMLE and indicates brainstem compression. Choice E: Medulloblastomas are aggressive midline posterior fossa tumors, most commonly arising in the cerebellar vermis of children. Choice F: Pinealomas arise in the pineal gland and classically cause Parinaud syndrome—vertical gaze palsy, light-near dissociation, and impaired accommodation—due to compression of the dorsal midbrain. prompt: Choices A, B and C: Detrusor underactivity, outlet obstruction, and overflow incontinence all cause impaired bladder emptying, but their mechanisms and presentations differ from this patient’s case. Detrusor underactivity occurs when the bladder muscle cannot generate enough contractile force during micturition, often due to diabetic autonomic neuropathy, spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, or chronic overdistension. Outlet obstruction, in contrast, results from narrowing of the bladder neck or urethra—seen in conditions such as urethral stricture, pelvic tumor, bladder neck contracture, or benign prostatic hyperplasia in men—which raises detrusor pressures during voiding and causes hesitancy, weak stream, and postvoid dribbling. Both detrusor underactivity and outlet obstruction can lead to overflow incontinence, in which bladder capacity is exceeded and urine dribbles constantly; this is accompanied by a distended bladder, high postvoid residual volumes, and often recurrent urinary tract infections. In this patient, postvoid residual volume is normal, bladder emptying is not impaired, and symptoms are triggered by urgency rather than continuous leakage. There is no history of hesitancy, weak stream, straining to void, or incomplete emptying, and the course is not suggestive of chronic bladder dysfunction. These features make detrusor underactivity, outlet obstruction, and overflow incontinence unlikely explanations for her presentation.

answer: Optic neuritis Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents as sudden, painless, complete monocular vision loss, often associated with a cherry-red spot on fundus exam and a history of vascular risk factors (e.g., atrial fibrillation, carotid atherosclerosis). It does not cause eye pain, afferent pupillary defect, or signs of demyelination elsewhere in the CNS, such as clonus or urinary incontinence. CRAO is an ophthalmologic emergency but is not associated with demyelinating plaques or young women. Choice B: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute, immune-mediated demyelinating polyneuropathy affecting the peripheral nervous system, with ascending weakness, areflexia, and possible autonomic instability. GBS does not involve central demyelination, optic neuritis, or upper motor neuron findings. It typically follows a gastrointestinal or respiratory infection (e.g., Campylobacter jejuni) and is managed with IVIG or plasmapheresis. GBS spares the optic nerve, which is part of the CNS. Choice C: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) results from demyelination of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), also seen in MS. INO is characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and nystagmus of the contralateral eye during attempted lateral gaze. Unlike optic neuritis, INO does not present with acute vision loss or pain; instead, patients complain of diplopia. Clinical examination shows preserved pupillary light reflex, distinguishing it from optic neuritis. INO is highly associated with MS but is not the best match for the patient's acute monocular visual loss and afferent pupillary defect. Choice D: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder targeting the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction, causing fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness and fatigability, especially of ocular and bulbar muscles. It causes ptosis and diplopia but does not present with optic nerve dysfunction, afferent pupillary defect, upper motor neuron signs, or MRI findings of white matter plaques. Treatment includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and immunosuppression, not disease-modifying therapy for demyelination. Bottom line: Optic neuritis is a highly testable initial manifestation of multiple sclerosis, presenting with painful, acute, monocular vision loss and a relative afferent pupillary defect. MS should be considered in young women with CNS demyelination symptoms, including upper motor neuron findings and urinary incontinence. Pathology Neurology (Demyelinating diseases) None

answer: Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion the preferred surgical option, physically separating the offending vessel from the nerve root to relieve compression. Other procedures include percutaneous radiofrequency rhizotomy or gamma knife radiosurgery, which selectively damage pain fibers to reduce symptoms. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Axonal demyelination of facial motor fibers produces an acute, lower motor neuron palsy of cranial nerve VII with forehead involvement, loss of nasolabial fold, impaired eye closure, hyperacusis from stapedius weakness, and possible loss of taste on the anterior two thirds of the tongue. Pathology involves inflammatory demyelination of the facial nerve within the facial canal, leading to conduction block and flaccid facial weakness rather than paroxysmal pain. Symptoms evolve over hours to days and are not characteristically stimulus-triggered. Neurologic examination is abnormal between episodes in true facial nerve palsy. The vignette shows shock-like, touch-precipitated facial pain with a normal exam between attacks, which does not fit facial motor fiber demyelination. Choice B: Compression of the geniculate ganglion affects the sensory and parasympathetic components of the facial nerve, often causing deep otalgia, altered taste, decreased lacrimation, and progressive facial weakness from involvement of adjacent motor fibers. Tumor-related compression typically produces gradually worsening, persistent deficits rather than brief, electric, trigger-induced pain spells. Examination frequently reveals facial asymmetry or sensory changes, and imaging often shows a mass along the facial nerve pathway. The case features normal interictal neurologic findings and stimulus-evoked paroxysms in the trigeminal distribution, which are not explained by geniculate ganglion compression. Choice D: Small-vessel ischemia of brainstem sensory nuclei reflects lacunar pathology from lipohyalinosis or microatheroma in long-standing hypertension or diabetes, producing sustained sensory loss, dysesthesias, or ataxic syndromes depending on the tract involved. Deficits are continuous, often with accompanying objective signs on examination, and onset correlates with an ischemic event rather than dozens of brief attacks per day. Pain from central post-stroke phenomena is typically persistent or waxing–waning, not seconds-long shocks triggered by light touch. A normal neurologic examination between episodes and stimulus sensitivity argue strongly against a lacunar ischemic mechanism. Choice E: Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion classically causes herpes zoster in the V1–V3 dermatomes, presenting with prodromal burning pain followed by a unilateral vesicular eruption on an erythematous base and possible ocular involvement with V1 disease. Pathogenesis involves latent varicella-zoster virus replicating within sensory neurons, leading to inflammation and persistent neuropathic pain that may evolve into postherpetic neuralgia. The clinical course features continuous or lingering pain rather than very brief, electric, triggerable paroxysms; skin findings are typical, and examination often reveals cutaneous lesions. The absence of rash and the paroxysmal, touch- precipitated pattern make zoster reactivation unlikely here. Bottom line: Trigeminal neuralgia most commonly results from microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root by an aberrant arterial loop, producing focal demyelination and ephaptic cross-talk between sensory fibers. This pathology converts normal tactile stimuli into intense paroxysmal pain, often precipitated by light touch or facial movement, and is managed with sodium channel–blocking anticonvulsants. Lipofuscin accumulates over time as a byproduct of lysosomal digestion of oxidized membrane lipids and proteins, forming yellow-brown perinuclear inclusions. Patients may report difficulty climbing stairs or rising from a chair, and systemic features like interstitial lung disease may be present. Unlike this case, polymyositis usually lacks a preceding traumatic trigger and would not localize weakness to the lower extremities alone without other constitutional or serologic findings. answer: Trochlear nerve palsy can occur due to increased intracranial pressure or brainstem lesions, but it does not cause ptosis or pupillary changes. Management focuses on addressing underlying causes, such as reducing intracranial pressure or treating infection/tumor. Choice B: The medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) is a brainstem tract coordinating horizontal eye movements between cranial nerves III and VI. Lesions cause internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), classically presenting as impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye with nystagmus of the abducting contralateral eye during horizontal gaze. INO is commonly seen in multiple sclerosis (younger adults) or brainstem stroke (older adults). The illustration would show failure of one eye to adduct past midline with preserved convergence. No ptosis, mydriasis, or 'down and out' position occurs. Treatment targets the underlying neurologic disorder. Choice D: The optic nerve (cranial nerve II) transmits visual information. Lesions produce monocular vision loss, relative afferent pupillary defect, reduced visual acuity, and possibly central scotoma or loss of color vision (as in optic neuritis). The illustration would show a loss of visual field in one eye, not abnormal eye movement or ptosis. Optic nerve damage occurs in demyelinating diseases (multiple sclerosis), ischemic optic neuropathy, or compressive lesions. Empiric treatment for optic neuritis is high-dose corticosteroids; other causes require disease-specific therapy. Choice E: The trochlear nerve (cranial nerve IV) innervates the superior oblique muscle, which primarily intorts and depresses the eye when adducted. A lesion results in vertical diplopia, worse when looking down and in (e.g., reading, descending stairs). Patients characteristically tilt their head away from the affected side to compensate. The illustration would show an eye slightly elevated when attempting downward gaze and head tilt to the contralateral side. Trochlear nerve palsy rarely produces a blown pupil or ptosis and is most commonly caused by trauma (due to its long intracranial course) or microvascular ischemia. Treatment is generally supportive, with corrective prism glasses or surgery for persistent diplopia. Bottom line: A "down and out" eye with ptosis and mydriasis after head trauma is classic for oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy, often due to direct injury, aneurysmal compression, or uncal herniation.
